sentences.text: Yeah.
Yes.
Yeah, so now I will-- I-- I won't recall exactly the sequence.
But what happened--
Yes, shoveling snow.
At the time already we had to have arm bands on-- on the left.
I think on the left, or I don't remember anymore, arm bands with the Star of David.
This was you-- if you were caught without it, it's death.
And then we got rations, right?
They took away this.
They took away that.
I remember myself, there was-- I mentioned it in the other town, there was a square again in Debica too.
And once a week, the farmers were coming to sell their fruits and things.
And we were not allowed.
So once I ventured, and I went to buy a bag of apples.
And then there was a German police, the regular police.
He saw me.
I had the arm band, and he took the bag away, of apples, you know, young girl.
Yes.
Yes, restricted.
You are not allowed to buy on the market.
You were not allowed this.
The-- the store was-- they brought it-- we still had probably-- I don't know anything about finances.
But she-- we probably were well off.
Because she, my stepmother was alone working.
She must have money, or--
We still owned the store.
Yes.
And-- but they brought in a-- a somebody, you know, it's a Volksdeutsche.
Somebody, a German, by whatever.
And he was assigned as a-- not a commissioner-- you know, he was getting a salary.
And he-- on the paper that he owned the store.
And we were working.
And he had-- we were working in the store.
And he was getting a salary or we were getting.
I don't know exactly how it was.
So my stepmother was-- we even had before the war, we had the-- a young guy sleeping and helping in the store.
And he worked.
There was a maid.
But this was all gone.
And I-- you know, there was a young girl working.
I started doing all the work.
And then they-- the rations.
Then people from every family was required to go to work for the Germans, either snow or different something.
And my father was away.
But then he came back.
I don't remember-- you know, that when they had the pact with Stalin, right--
He was-- the Russians moved in.
Yes, so he was there.
He was not sent to-- to Siberia.

But these people that wanted to go back, the people, the refugees, right, to the east when they wanted to go back home, and they signed that they wanted to go back, repatriate, they were sent to Siberia.
Because they were enemies of the Communists.
Right?
My father was there, and he came back later.
And then he had some job.
My brother was going to work.
I don't know exactly what they were doing in factories and--
We were still living in our house.
There was still no ghetto.
There was policemen.
There was-- I know there was a young guy that was in love with me, whatever.
He was working-- I've heard that he was such a kapo later, a very entrepreneur.
And he was working for the Germans.
He spoke German.
And they liked him.
I don't know what he was doing there, and he was bringing us slabs of-- he was in Denmark.
I have to remember, because I remember the taste of it, Danish butter, big slabs of Danish butter.
So they already had Denmark.
And they were taking everything.
But since this was a small town, and the neighboring villages, and we had-- so we were not so bad off with food yet.
We could always buy some potatoes or the bread was rationed, but--
Yes--
After or maybe a year later, maybe 1940.
And then they were coming, and they were taking more.
And then they restricted.
I don't know when they took away the store.
I know that there was a kind of a German who-- what is it?
He was put as the-- the-- the what is the right word?
The owner, he became the owner.
Yes, yes and then my stepmother and something, we had to work in the store, and I don't know how many things.
And now I-- my stepmother still in the beginning probably, maybe 1940 already.
There was no school.
We couldn't go to school.
And we also had-- that's you know, I'm talking about these feelings.
And now I appreciate her so much more that I didn't know when I was a child.
She took a-- they were right after the Germans marched in, and they came to Lodz.
And they-- there were refugees from Lodz, very well-to-do people.
Well, she took one.
There was a guy that finished the Hebrew gymnasium.
And he had a-- so he was a tutor for me.
I studied, you know, gymnasium and Hebrew.
A refugee, not to take in.
But she paid him for the lessons, a tutor--
--a refugee from Lodz.
Yeah.
I have to mention that I also was taking bookkeeping lessons with a former principal of the gymnasium.
Yes, yes, no.
This was going to her for lessons.
I don't remember.
But maybe she recommended me to someone that worked in the-- in the office, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] economy-- I don't know how to-- to-- [INAUDIBLE], whatever.
And he had privately, he lived in a Jewish big house, right.
And he had-- he established a bookkeeping office.
And I got a job there.
So this was my-- I was going to work.
I was coming, working at home.
But I was employed.
No, this was a Polish-- Polish maybe probably German-speaking or whatever.
And I was going to work there, and I was Polish people from Posen, you know.
Somebody was the head of the office.
I-- they treated me OK.
I was like assistant bookkeeper, or whatever.
I was coming home.
And this gave me the right.
I had papers that I was employed.
So when they finally made the ghetto, right--
It was probably in 1941 or beginning '42.
Yeah.
Yeah.
But I don't know how to convey this slow, slow squeezing.
It didn't start right away everything.
I remember still in the house, they cut off the electricity.
There was no electricity.
And we had some kind of lamps, not kerosene even, some other kind of lamp that was-- I don't know how we heated the houses.
Because with the stoves, whatever.
And then I don't know exactly when, maybe end of '41 or something, when they established the ghetto.
It must have been '42.
So I told you whatever I remembered.
I was working.
My father was working, my brother were going to work.
And they were--
It was still-- this was not the ghetto yet.
We are still the ghetto.
Then came the first, this action, what be called aktion in Polish, right?
They-- we were not in the ghetto.
I don't know exactly, if this was already the ghetto, or not yet.
Maybe they made this ghetto after this action, I think, after.
Now I-- also my mother's father lived with us at the time, the grandfather.
Because he was then, his wife died.
And this was what was so poignant, you know, talking about-- about not resisting.
There was no way to resist.
Yes.
So came the day, it was a announcement-- this was-- I guess it was already in '42 or '41.
Probably it must have been '42.
Yeah, must have been '42.
In German they had-- they announced that everybody has to stay home.
They-- not to go out.
And they brought then people, then my grandmother, right, the one that I was with them when the Germans moved in.
And in the beginning, I was still able to go and visit to my-- or maybe not during the war, in the beginning of the war.
Anyhow, they surrounded the whole thing, and nobody was allowed to-- it was that even nobody was allowed to leave, all-- all the night.
And we were staying the following day.
They-- so we were sitting.
We didn't know if we've going to live or not, from the next day.
Because this has already started, this.
They brought people, Jews, with wagons, with horses, with the carriages from the neighboring smaller town and villages to this Debica.
And my father came from this other, from [INAUDIBLE], and many of his friends, relatives, came.
And they all came, you know-- I am a little mixed up.
Maybe this was already after the ghetto.
Wait a minute.
Yeah, they brought everybody in, right, from the surrounding things.
And my grandmother came home, came and she was all bent.
I remember her walking to the house.
The son was supporting her.
Her daughter-in-law and her daughter and their little baby, two years old, a little beautiful doll, beautiful doll with them.
And I see her walking bent, with the-- with the cane.
And then she lie down on the bed, and she slept.
And I felt, you know, the first time, my-- my-- my heart, how I was sorry for her.
You know, and she was-- and she was old.
And then they had to go to-- they called the people, whoever had papers was allowed to stay, right, in the house.
So I had papers.
My stepmother was working outside.
My father and all the others had to-- to leave.
And I remember the old people, right.
Now my-- my stepmother's father was a religious [? older, ?] I see it still now, he wasn't allowed to stay, right?
And this was his daughter.
But the fear of being caught, or maybe she wanted to protect, and he was facing her.
He wanted her to-- to hide him.
That was a house that used to probably belong to some people that had horses or whatever.
Because we had an outside, a big thing for wood and for coal, right.
And she could have-- somebody was hiding there.
But they were found out, you know, behind-- because piles of wood for the winter.
And she was facing him and she wouldn't-- you know, she didn't know what to do.
So she had to let him go.
She was so-- everybody was so helpless.
She had her children to protect.
Because we took everything so literally when they said, you're not allowed to go out, you're not allowed to shelter anybody.
Because we will be killed.
So all of these people--
All of these people--
--without papers, were rounded up.
Were rounded up, and my father-- this was the first aktion.
My-- my stepmother was working.
They took my father too, and the two little, her children, the two step-children, they were at that time probably five and three or something, or four and six.
She took them, and they were in some-- some such a godly place, right.
It a big thing.
We-- I never saw it.
The gestapo had a little, like a table set up.
And everybody was coming with their papers.
And it was already this rejection, like in the camps.
I didn't see it.
I was told about it.
Because I was home.
Whoever can stay, whoever--
So she took the children, and she went to this place to plead with the gestapo for the father, to my father, to something.
And when this gestapo saw their little children, they were blonde and blue eyes, really Aryan or something like I saw in Sweden.
So he picked them up and he, said, oh children that look like this deserve to have a father.
And they were able to take my father back from this-- this-- this-- this-- platz, whatever this was, home.
And I've heard later that my grandmother and the son and the daughter-in-law and the daughter had to leave.
They left behind, without our knowledge, the little girl.
The mother left her, you know, that we should take care.
Because she knew that we were staying.
And I was told later that old people were taken on trucks, and they were taking them to the mountain.
We were already in the Carpathian mountains.
This was the south of Poland.
And they were probably pushing her up there on the truck that her son interfered, and he yelled at this whoever it was, don't you push my mother.
So this guy took out the gun, and he shot the grandmother and shot him right in front of her daughter and the wife.
This is what I was told.
This was, the aktion is over.
I don't think I was working anymore.
But the time was already very-- you know, then they created the ghetto.
And in our house, a little house, there were maybe my father was there, maybe 23 people from his something.
And I don't know how, sleeping on the floors.
And-- and the women were taking care of this little girl.
And then came in November, I think, there came another action.
So now, the child-- we went to hiding for a few days, the Polish neighbors took us in with our children.
We took the little two-year-old girl.
But we couldn't, because she was crying.
So in the house, in the bedroom, we had a little that you opened in the floor.
And they were going down to the cellar, where we kept potatoes and things.
So some people were hiding there, and took the little girl.
This was-- I was told this later-- that she was crying.
The Polish police came and took her.
And they probably shot her.
And I still see her in this blue velvet dress like a little doll playing.
And my-- my brother, I think, was taken away on the train.
It was a train heading east somewhere.
I think this was this train that was taking them to [?
Belzitz. ?] Maybe this was the train where they gassed people in the train.
No And no, and even the first action, we were in the house.
And we heard shooting, and we heard these mens were shot, and this one was shot, and whatever was taken.
But somehow I never witnessed this.
Because I was in the house.
Yes.
Now next, people started to have to save themselves.
Some people-- so I had this-- you know, we were going out with my stepmother, from the ghetto.
I think it wasn't closed yet, which were the boundaries.
And we were going to my boss, who was an important figure in this Debica, In that township or something.
And he was willing to help out.
You know, we were bringing him things from the house.
We had some beautiful-- whatever, linens or--
Yes.
Silk, I remember this was handmade, whatever.
And women started-- you know, started also this-- the Poles were being sent to work in Germany, like conscription, right.
Every something from the village had to give so many people to work to Germany.
And the people started somehow procured some false papers.
I know some people were running away.
But there was not too much communication.
You know, our neighbors were gone.
There was a-- at the time there were neighbors that had a bakery.
There was a-- a guy that had his legs amputated.
His best friend had to take him out to be shot, and he was pleading with them to shot him in the head, or whatever.
But they just did the opposite.
So what happened to me, I had no papers, right?
And I've heard that one of the girls went to this gathering, whatever.
And she was recognized that she was Jewish by the Polish people that she was sent back.
So I think it was my-- this boss that had this idea, or I had this idea.
I wanted to run away to go to Germany to do something.
My father was home.
And there was nothing that I could help my family.
There was nothing they could do for me.
So I just wanted to take a-- a chance.
There was no risk.
Because the risk was, I believed that-- that nobody will survive.
Yes.
So, [DRINKS] we're going there.
And somehow how to-- to join this group, you know, I wanted to go away to-- to Germany to survive.
But I had no name.
I had no-- I don't even know, we didn't know anybody who would do the paper.
But I know people that survived with papers.
So one day the-- the-- ghetto was already, we were already out of our house.
Because after the second action, there were less people that they wouldn't allow them their room, right?
Everything was closed, right.
And I remember only that the German soldiers were coming and going.

And I remember this is important, the little-- our children right, that the little-- the two, and the little girl and the boy, when they saw the soldiers how they were running like mice, you know, running away.
They had already this-- this fear.
And one day I-- somebody called me to the gate, to the gate or to the-- it was a barbed wire around this ghetto.
And came my-- our neighbor, the close neighbor, the ones that my father always helped out.
The husband was an alcoholic.
He was a painter.
They were always-- before the war, they were buying things.
They didn't pay for it, and something.
We were close.
And we were giving them things too, from the house, whatever.
She brought me a little notice from my boss, [? who ?] I did work for.
And he told me, so this is I never met him after the war, and I'm still grateful.
He told me since he had connections in the-- in the township right-- that at 2 o'clock Sunday afternoon there will be a transport of the people going to Germany.
They will march through the railroad street, near our house to the railroad station.
If I could join it, while they walk, right, then maybe I can somehow-- it was a crazy idea.
Because I didn't, you know, these people had papers, right?
Assignments.
So you had no papers, and you got this message now.
No, I had no name.
And I had no papers.
And I just [INAUDIBLE], you know, that's all.
And my father, you know, they agreed that if I want to save myself.
Because there was nothing to-- to hold me.
And if I may say so, because I was already independent, I wasn't so attached.
You know, maybe I was more independent.
That I chose to-- to follow this.
And it was easy.
You see, I'm apologizing.
It was easier for me to leave my family maybe for other kids that were so close they wouldn't leave their parents.
Yes, yes, yes.
So my stepmother packed a suitcase.
I still had a nice sweater, good things, and it was decided they helped that this Mrs.
Klimashevska and a friend of hers, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] would-- would help me to-- to walk with me to the railroad station.
So I had this thing.
And then I, let's say, there was a cellar with the little windows in our house.
And the neighbor lived around the house in a Jewish abandoned home.
And so I mentioned it, it wasn't as strict as in the big ghetto in Krakow or Warsaw.
So I jumped from the window, because it was a one-story house, into the garden, and walked to this-- my neighbor.
Right?
They gave me food.
They give me to eat.
I had my suitcase, and already then I opened it.
I had the nice sweater, handmade sweater, they-- so they took it, you know.
They took this sweater.
But then-- Now this was all at the suggestion of your boss?
Yes, yes.
Sado.
S-A-D-O.
I don't remember his first name, Pan Sado.
I never saw him again.
That's a good something.
And--
I think a few days before, since this was already like the idea was germinating, right?
My neighbor, they bleached my hair with peroxide to be blonde, right?
I had my hair was bleached.
And I had a kerchief on the head, took off this.
I went to them, right?
When the time came, and my-- my father, this is the last time I saw his eyes following me around, you know, to the neighbors.
And in the letter the Mr.
Sado, right, wrote that there will be a Polish policeman.
I forgot his name, who knew my father.
Because we were all-- you know, the police they're probably getting some money or whatever that he will be one of the-- the companion guards or--
Escort, escort.
There was German police.
They were mostly Ukrainian police.
They were the worst.
They had black uniforms.
So this man was, I didn't see him that he will know that I will be there.
That he was told that I will be there.
So--
So this was-- no, this was still in this Debica, and I'm with the neighbor.
And this was winter, but there was no snow.
And this is the railroad, a road that was leading to the-- you walked to the railroad station.
And so this Mrs.
Klimashevska and her friend, another neighbor, we walked out and they-- they held me on both sides.
And we walked.
And here the transport was going with the middle of the road.
And we were on the sidewalk.
OK.
